title: What I Think About Bike Touring 4/4

transcript.text: Okay, next video of Biketune part 4 because I forgot to include some other stuff.
Like Biketune, see, once everybody starts making websites like me, like mine, like the one I'm going to make,
Where you totally record everything, like how long it took me to get from point A to point B,
People are going to see my website, right?
Or not just my website, but everybody's websites, and everybody's going to know the record.
Like how long it took the guy to go like over, you know, cross this river and go over the pass to the next river.
And it was like, yeah, so-and-so did it in like 12 hours, man.
So then you can be like, oh yeah, and then you do it.
And then you go, oh yeah, I did it at 11.30, woohoo, new record, new record.
And, you know, in order to make it so you don't lie, you know, I mean, you just do your video camera.
And you push the video camera, here I am crossing the river.
And then you push again, here I am crossing the other river.
And you can see in the time on the thing, right?
And you can't tweak that.
So anyways, it'll just make it really interesting that way.
And there'll be like, you know, an infinite amount of different courses.
So anybody can have a record for something, you know.
But obviously there's going to be some that are more prestigious than others, you know.
Because there's some rides that are just amazing, like crisscrossing New Zealand or, you know, getting up over, say, bike touring up the Yant La Luzuez or something like that.
And then, of course, then people will start bike touring really lightly.
And with the whole couchsurfing thing, you know, it'll be so easy that people can just be able to camp at anybody's house, you know.
They'll be like houses where everybody knows you can bike tour camp because people like the bike tourists.
Because normally bike tourists are really, you know, they're hardworking, you know, trustworthy people.
Well, anyway, so anybody will let them stay there.
So, yeah, the future of bike touring, I think, is going to be really big.
And then even bike tour groups, people hook up just on the road, wherever they are, because there's so many bike tours now.
You have, like, bike tours, pace lines and stuff.
And this is kind of similar to, like, oh, man, here I am eating again.
It's so disgusting when you eat on the video, sorry.
You, like, with the bike race, like, there's 24 hours in my lab that I did as part of the group.
They have, like, records, like, how far you can ride in one day in a 24-hour period.
And, like, the people who win it, they don't break at all.
They ride 24 hours straight, literally.
Like, they go, every lap they do, I think the guy's record is, like, 16 laps, and the girl's record is, like, 13 laps.
Of these, like, each lap, it takes, like, an hour or something to go around.
And then they sign it, you know, and then they go again.
So it's, like, it's pretty amazing.
And I've never actually ridden that far straight without stopping.
But, so, that would be trippy.
And, you know, normally I don't think any of those other people have.
Maybe those other people have either.
They only do it at the race, you know, where they're all motivated to do it because everybody's there together.
But with bike touring, you can motivate yourself to do it if it's, like, a record from here to here and it took 24 hours, you know.
Or it's, like, if you can do this in less than 24 hours, I'm the first guy to do that in under 24 hours or something.
And you can get motivated to do 24-hour rides by yourself and, you know, not need other people to do it.
But, but we'll see, you know, because I think with this 24-hour bike race back in the past, it was, like, only one guy who did it.
And then it was, like, a few guys, you know.
And then it was one girl.
Now it's a few girls and stuff.
Actually, when I did it, I did it part of a group.
I'll tell you my story of it because it was pretty fun.
They chose me to be the first guy to start my team.
So then I start.
And I haven't touched my bike for two months because I worked in, well, tried to work in Sydney for a month and I was working in Colorado for a month.
But I was still, before that I had bike toured through Australia, so I was still pretty fit, you know.
I just didn't have the leg speed.
So, anyway, they do the thing, the gun, and I run around and I'm the very first person to go on the bike.
I'm the very first person to run up the trail.
I figure, you know, I'm not here to win or anything, so I might as well have my sun, light sun, have my sunshine just for the fun of it, just for the beginning.
And then I blow up and everybody passes me like I'm, like, not even moving.
And then all this dusk is kicked up and I, like, kind of get an asthma attack, but it's not really a bad asthma attack.
It's just, like, and then, and before the race started, I looked around and see who the strongest girl was.
I figured, I figured I'd maybe be good, but good competition for the strongest girl in the state that I was in at that time.
And I saw this one, like, freakishly skinny, like, girl with really long blonde hair with, like, dreadlocks or, like, braids or something like that.
And I figured she'd be the strongest, so I was kind of keeping an eye out for her.
And then, yeah, sure enough, she comes up and I'm, like, riding kind of, like, with her and her group.
And I crashed on one, going around one bin, but then I get up and nobody passes me.
And then I'm riding, then that girl actually, like, rides away from me on this flat two-track section right on the back, right before this last hill.
But then I turn around and I see this other girl, and she's, like, really, like, hot.
And, like, she's, like, 30 feet behind me, so I, like, slow down to let her, like, get on my wheel.
And I'm kind of, like, looking back, like, every, like, five seconds to make sure she's still on my wheel.
You know, and I slow up a little bit when I ride away from her.
Like, she wasn't really fast on that flat section.
And then I pulled her up the hill, the climb, and then down the whole backside.
And somebody goes, oh, that's the first girl, that's the first girl.
I guess they didn't see the other girl who was in front of her.
And she goes, yeah, and I'm doing the whole thing solo, too.
I was, like, oh, man, that's hot.
So we pull in, and she, you know, like, kind of, like, grins at me.
Goes off on her second lap.
I was, like, holy shit.
And then, like, my other teammates did it.
Like, these two guys and this girl.
And it was my turn to go.
And by now, it was nighttime.
So then I do it.
And, like, yeah, like, there was, like, all these people in this trail.
And it was, like, this big old party.
Like, all these people, like, hey, yeah, you go, go, go.
Even at nighttime.
It was, like, 9 or 10 at night or something like that.
And it was cool.
And I was doing really well, actually.
I was, like, totally kicking ass.
I felt like I actually had some leg speed on me.
And I wasn't having the asthma because there was no dust up.
And there was no, like, crowds of people everywhere, like, in your way.
And I go up the backside on the climb.
And then both of my chain rings fall off.
My big chain rings.
Because this was, like, my brother's bike.
But I loved it, too.
It was a fat chance.
I totally loved that bike.
It was the awesomeest bike ever.
And, but, yeah, those things come off.
So, then I have to practically, like, coast down the entire backside.
And, but I ended up doing really well.
I think it was, like, 10 minutes, 10 or 15 minutes faster than my daytime lap.
But then, like, I go to those, and then I go to those guys.
And I'm, like, yeah, you're turning.
And they were, like, oh, we were just going to sleep.
We don't want to go.
We don't want to do it.
So, I was, like, oh, all right.
I'll do another lap on your bike.
So, I did another lap on my teammate's bike.
But, but I had to, like, shorten, like, lower the seat, like, all these times.
Like, like a dozen times I had to stop on the side of the road and lower the seat again with a wrench.
And, and every time it was still too high.
And I felt like I'd lowered it, like, a foot, and the freaking seat was still too high.
It was, and it was the worst bike.
It had the worst handling.
It freaked me out.
And I finally go home, and I'm, like, I hate your bike.
I can't ride it ever again.
Go home.
And, like, none of my teammates wanted to go.
They wanted to sleep.
And I was, like, well, I'm not going.
I had the opportunity to go and do the whole thing solo because my teammates didn't want to do it.
But I couldn't do it on that bike, man.
That bike was fucking pissing me off.
So, anyway, we just hung out and, like, watched it the next day.
But it looked really cool, like, all these people there.
And, and the guys who, like, won it, I think I saw some of the guys who were, like, top two or three or whatever.
And they were, like, they were, like, the guy would stop and he would, like, take a drink of his water and, like, hang out with his friends for, like, a minute it seemed like.
And then go off on his other lap.
So, I don't know what that was about.
Maybe you actually do have to rest.
But, um, but anyways, uh, I am, I don't know how those guys train.
But my, my point is that bike touring is the one, is the, is the best way to train for that.
As long as when you're bike touring, you're, you're making sure that you're riding a 24-hour ride at least on the, every month on the full moons.
And maybe two for the full moons.
One, one when the moon is half full, becoming full.
And then one when it's half full, becoming not full.
Or something.
But anyways, the cool thing about bike touring is that you just record the whole thing.
And then, you know, one guy does, does a bike tour, you get to check out his website.
And then you go, okay, cool, I want to do that, only I want to do it even better.
And it kind of just grows on each other like that.
But anyways, I'm, like, uh, I'm, like, out of time again.
So that's part two, bike touring kicks ass.
I'm going to do it.
Stay tuned.
In two months I'm going to go.
All right.
